rules.rule: CancellationToken must be the last parameter in every method signature.
CancellationToken must default to `default` — never omit the default value.
CancellationToken must not be nullable (CancellationToken? is forbidden).
The parameter must be named cancellationToken — abbreviated names (ct, token, cancelToken) are forbidden.
CancellationToken should be accepted by methods that perform database, HTTP, file, queue/messaging, long-running, orchestration fan-out, or parallel operations.
CancellationToken must not be added to trivial in-memory methods that perform no I/O and have no waiting behaviour.
The same token must flow through every layer. The token must never be silently dropped.
A new CancellationTokenSource must not be created to replace an upstream token. Use CreateLinkedTokenSource when a timeout must be added.
When timeout behaviour is required, tokens must be linked using CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource(cancellationToken, timeoutSource.Token).
Any method that accepts a CancellationToken must call cancellationToken.ThrowIfCancellationRequested() before executing dependency or long-running operations.
When a timeout source exists, catch (OperationCanceledException) when (timeoutSource.IsCancellationRequested) must always appear before the plain catch (OperationCanceledException) block.
A TimeoutException produced from a timeout-triggered OperationCanceledException must be wrapped and treated as a dependency failure.
OperationCanceledException must never be wrapped in any other exception type. It must always be rethrown with throw;.
When a method uses CancellationToken but has no timeout differentiation, catch (OperationCanceledException) must not be present.
The same CancellationToken must be passed to every parallel task in a Task.WhenAll fan-out.
Dependency exception tests must include TimeoutException in the Theory MemberData.
Tests must verify that a true cancellation results in OperationCanceledException propagating unwrapped.
